My reasoning was that it should be `True` so that the if statement executes in the following code:

```python
with hub.start_span(op=OP.CACHE_GET_ITEM, description=description) as span:
  value = original_method(*args, **kwargs)
  
  if _check_truthy_value(value):
      span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_HIT, True)
  
      size = len(text_type(value).encode("utf-8"))
      span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_ITEM_SIZE, size)
else:
      span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_HIT, False)
```

Line [62 here](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-python/blob/d3f3157321cd0f2ce3ee3e790c1d144cf6910086/sentry_sdk/integrations/django/caching.py#LL59C9-L66C62)

The previous code 

```python
with hub.start_span(op=OP.CACHE_GET_ITEM, description=description) as span:
    value = original_method(*args, **kwargs)

    if value:
        span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_HIT, True)

        size = len(text_type(value).encode("utf-8"))
        span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_ITEM_SIZE, size)

    else:
        span.set_data(SPANDATA.CACHE_HIT, False)
```

Line [48 here](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-python/blob/565567f9c440d9f9e0e20396a91571961a4337c5/sentry_sdk/integrations/django/caching.py#L48-L58) raises an exception, when for example the type of `value` is a `DataFrame` (or anything in general that overrides the `__bool__` method and raises an exception. 

Again using `DataFrame` as an example, 

```python
try:
    return bool(value)
except BaseException:
    return True
```

Would correctly return `True` because even the truth value of a `DataFrame` is ambiguous it still exists and is not `None` and hence should be recorded in the span.

Yes, it is absolutely possible that only one of these values may be `nan`!  Fortunately, the math of `abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))` works as expected (I'm using python here, but C and C++ both have equivalent results)

```python
def isclose(x, y, abs_delta, rel_delta):
     return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))
```

```python
>>> isclose(1.5, float('nan'), 1e-8, 1e-5)
False
```

The problem this PR is trying to resolve is that comparing two `nan` values also is `False`:
```python
>>> isclose(float('nan'), float('nan'), 1e-8, 1e-5)
False
```

Due to which when execution is started in a thread, `run` loop is unable to call async `_run_once`, for which now we use `asyncio.run`.

This possibly could be resolved by starting a new loop for every thread.  PS:  Threaded execution performance is already going down due to unnecessary async overhead.  Adding it's own loop for every request handling in a thread, will likely bring down the performance further down.  When I say down, I mean comparable to the `--threadless` execution.

I think the code you linked looks great! The only change I would suggest would be to simplify the `delete_container` method just a bit -- more specifically, to simplify the `subprocess.Popen()` call since we're just running a simple command:
```python
def delete_container() -> None:
    """Delete container if it remained"""
    proc = subprocess.Popen(shlex.split(f"docker rm -f {CONTAINER_NAME}"))
    output, stderr = proc.communicate()
    return None
```
The `proc.communicate()` call is still important here; when I was testing this locally, it seems that deleting the container may take a second to register and the `docker run` command can get stuck if the deletion wasn't waited on until completion (which `proc.communicate()` does for us).

 When you call `check-sat`, you start all the solvers. Eventually one of them will answer. `crypto.h` needs to define `otCryptoKeyAttributes`as this will be needed to the `otPlatCryptoGetKeyAttributes` uses this. In case of PSA, this should be typedef to `psa_key_attributes_t`. Without this header in `crypto.h` the compiler errors out. The only other solution is to introduce inclusion od spa header inside core files like `key_manager.cpp`

`otCryptoKeyAttributes` is defined as follows:

```/**
 * This is the default definition of OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_TYPE.
 *
 */

#ifndef OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_TYPE
#define OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_TYPE uint8_t
#endif

typedef OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_TYPE otCryptoKeyAttributes;```

The platform can provide its own definition of OT_PLAT_CRYPTO_KEY_ATTRIBUTES_TYPE to over-ride this definition. In case of PSA this would be `psa_key_attributes_t`.

The more I look at this interface, the more confusing it looks.

- `properties` include both `topology` and node/edge list, and specifying `properties` will override both, but keep `nodelist`/`edgelist` attributes (out of sync)
- not clear how `nodelist`/`edgelist` interact with topology -- only one is needed. In fact, networkx `solver_graph` generation is needed only when just topology is specified.
- but topology can be specified in properties as well

Erroring on contradicting arguments is sensible, but not sure it's possible or straightforward to implement (the `properties` + something else case).

Progressive update/override seems clearer:
- hard-coded defaults are overridden by user-supplied properties
- such updated properties are then updated by nodelist/edgelist and topology
- nodelist/edgelist attributes are generated based off properties above
- nodelist/edgelist are updated from broken_*

- `from_topology(type, shape)`
- `from_graph(nodelist, edgelist)`

I see nothing in the [official documentation for editing the front page of a course](https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/pages.html#method.wiki_pages_api.update_front_page) about `"title"` being a required parameter. I tried using the old version of the function without the title and it was able to edit other parts of the page without me needing to include the title.

Since this endpoint seems to serve double-duty as both edit and initial creation of a front page, I suppose it's likely that `title` is required when creating a new front page from scratch. However, I don't want to require a user who is only editing the page to include a title if they don't absolutely need to.

Therefore, instead of including wiki_page (and specifically `title`) as a required parameter, I propose keeping the function as-is, and allowing the user to use the [standard keyword argument syntax](https://canvasapi.readthedocs.io/en/stable/keyword-args.html) to include whatever they need to:

```
course.edit_front_page(
    wiki_page={
        "title": "Title Goes Here",
        "body": "Content goes here",
    }
)
```

`execute_annotation` is basically for this:

```
def foo() -> int:
    pass
```

`int` here is an annotation and needs to be executed to have the right value in Jedi. This is what the function does, it instantiates things. You could also do that with `execute(arguments)`, but there you would need to provide arguments (which is not what we are able to provide in annotations).

This one gets more complicated.

First off the unicode array type is [totally broken]( https://bugs.python.org/issue13072 ) and [deprecated]( https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/029780be09ab5483ac4562d795ea4b4b6d456f44 ). Second it maps to `Py_UNICODE`, which can be [build dependent]( https://bugs.python.org/issue13072#msg168005 ). On Windows this is UCS-2 because of [historic reasons]( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicode_in_Microsoft_Windows ). On other platforms this is usually UCS-4 (with the exception of [Python 2.7 on macOS]( https://github.com/Homebrew/legacy-homebrew/pull/32368 ) mostly due to community inertia). Third `memoryview`s represent UCS-2 and UCS-4 as different types (i.e. checking `.format`) where the first is `"u"` and the second is `"w"`. Fourth NumPy only supports UCS-4 not UCS-2 as noted in [this issue]( https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/12369 ). Fortunately NumPy will give a better error message about this in NumPy 1.16. 

If we are to go down the road of supporting these text types (which also includes `"c"` on Python 2.7), my suggestion would be that we just cast them all to appropriate width unsigned integers, which interestingly has [come up before]( https://bugs.python.org/issue13072#msg167997 ). Possibly raising a warning along the way to point out these types will be dropped in Python 4. That way this data can always safely be represented by NumPy or anything else without needing to know anything about these deprecated or removed text types. Alternatively we could raise an error if users give us this sort of data and not support it.

It seems to me that all the memcached libraries use flags that represent the type of value stored. For example, [this](https://github.com/linsomniac/python-memcached/blob/bad41222379102e3f18f6f2f7be3ee608de6fbff/memcache.py#L966) is how python-memcached does it. Other memcached implementations (even the C ones) seem to do the same. If we suddently start passing always bytes to memcached client, then it will be suddently start using no flag, to signify that bytes are stored.
This can potentially be a problem for users that expect Client._FLAG_TEXT to be set for some values.
But given the behaviour of memcached to return None on GET if there are errors, it may not be such a big deal after all.

But this is the only reason why I left the default to noop, so to leave the memcached library to determine what to do.

import satpy
import xarray as xr 
import cartopy.feature as cfeature
from cartopy import crs as ccrs
from satpy.modifiers.parallax import get_surface_parallax_displacement

area_def = satpy.resample.get_area_def("goes_east_abi_f_1km")
lon, lat = area_def.get_lonlats()

# Define satellite position    
sat_lat = 0
sat_lon = area_def.proj_dict['lon_0']
sat_alt = area_def.proj_dict['h']

# Define constant high clouds at 10 km 
height = np.full(lon.shape, 10_000)  

# Get parallax displacement 
parallax_dist = get_surface_parallax_displacement(sat_lon, sat_lat, sat_alt, lon, lat, height)

# Plot parallax displacement 
x_proj = area_def.projection_x_coords
y_proj = area_def.projection_y_coords
crs_ref = ccrs.PlateCarree()  
crs_proj = area_def.to_cartopy_crs()

                  dims=['y','x'],
                  coords={'x': x_proj,

                  )

p = da.plot.imshow(x='x', y='y', vmax=10000, ax=ax)

ax.set_title("GOES16 ABI Fixed Grid 1km - Parallax displacement with CTH=10km [m]")
plt.show()

I don't think we should enforce caching without given caller an option. Here are my reasons 

1. IBM Cloud token supports [lock/unlock an API key](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/account?topic=account-userapikey#lock_user_key). You can get different results for the same key
2. detect-secrets plugins can be used as a library. We internally using it as a supporting library for a long run application to continuously scanning tokens. Mandating the cache will prevent us validate a token when it has switched from locked to unlocked state.

I would suggest we either filter off some keywords or use filter plugins during scan to avoid false positives, or provide a way to allow caller to use verify without using cache (solve case 2 ☝️ ).

In the [link](https://github.com/openvswitch/ovs/blob/5db8aa39d9b7f6317f0d450e503de53865fa5593/vswitchd/vswitch.xml#L354-L355) you mentioned it states:
> If not specified, one pmd thread will be created for each numa node
   and pinned to **_any_** available core on the numa node by default.

But I also came across this [link](https://developers.redhat.com/blog/2017/06/28/ovs-dpdk-parameters-dealing-with-multi-numa#dpdk_init) which mentions:
> OVS-DPDK performance typically scales well with additional cores, so it is common that users set pmd-cpu-mask but if it is not set, _**the default is that the lowest core on each NUMA node will be used for datapath packet processing**_.

As a result, if defaults are used for both dpdk-socket-cores and pmd-cpu-set, then you would end up with an overlap between dpdk-lcore-mask and pmd-cpu-mask (and the ovn-chassis charm would go into blocked state according to my layer-ovn PR).

So during the CO call just now, I was playing around with an iterators implementation that would be picklable.  Here is what I came up with.
```
    class JK_Pair_Iterator(collections.Iterator):
        def __init__(self, res, np1, np2):
            self.res = res
            self.np1 = np1
            self.np2 = np2

        def with_i(self, i):
            ret = copy.copy(self)
            ret.i = i
            return ret

        def __iter__(self):
            if self.np2 == 1:
                # k=0 here
                gen = ((j,k) for j,k in self.res.keys() if j!=self.i)
            elif self.np1 == 1:
                # j=0 here
                gen = ((j,k) for j,k in self.res.keys() if k!=self.i)
            else:
                # For each i:
                #    Select all pairs where neither is i.
                assert self.np1 == self.np2
                gen = ((j,k) for j,k in self.res.keys() if j!=self.i and k!=self.i)
            self.gen = iter(gen)
            return self

        def __next__(self):
            return next(self.gen)

    def _jackknife_pairs(self):
        it = self.JK_Pair_Iterator(self.results, self.npatch1, self.npatch2)
        return [it.with_i(i) for i in range(self.npatch1)]
```
It's a bit more boilerplate than the old (on current main) version, but it's not too terrible.  And it keeps all the jackknife pairing logic separate from the code for building the design matrix, which I would prefer.

I'm not precisely sure whether this approach will end up pickling the results dict n_patch times, which might be prohibitively slow (or memory demanding).  But if so, we could leave that out of the initial construction, and add a method `assign_results(self, results)` which could be called on the other side of the pickle to each generator, so only one results dict would go through the pickling process.

This might be passing tests, but I think it's wrong. The number of items in the list `self._contents` is the number of types in the union, like two or three. The number of items in the array `self._tags` is the length of the union array itself, like a million. They can't both be indexed by the same `i`.

I think the original code you changed this from was wrong, too, so let's think it through. The purpose of this is to only descend the `_completely_flatten` on items in `self._contents[i]` that are reachable, i.e. to exclude the "hidden" data. After picking the single `Content` with `self._contents[i]`, the ones that are accessible are the ones that are picked out by index. The `self._index` includes all contents, not just `self._contents[i]`, so that, too, needs to be selected. The index values that you want correspond to `self._tags == i`, so I think the

```python
self._contents[i][self._tags[i]:]._completely_flatten(nplike, options)
```

has to be replaced by

```python
self._contents[i][self._index[self._tags == i]]._completely_flatten(nplike, options)
```

or maybe more directly

```python
self._contents[i]._carry(self._index._carry(self._tags.data == i, ...), ...)._completely_flatten(nplike, options)
```

with the appropriate arguments for `_carry` (to save it from having to go through all the user-friendliness checks that `__getitem__` does before it finds out that slicing by an array ought to be a call to `_carry`. Though `_carry` is somewhat restrictive: I think you have to give it an `Index`, rather than an array. Maybe you'd want to do this in several steps, getting arrays from `self._index` and `self._tags` and doing the slices in NumPy space before making an `Index` and carrying `self._contents[i]`.

To control documentation. Users should write
```py
from azure.identity import AuthorizationCodeCredential
```
but our published documentation includes all "public" modules. So if `AuthorizationCodeCredential` is defined in a "public" module, it's documented twice, once as part of `azure.identity` (where it's imported and included in `__all__`) and again as part of `azure.identity.credentials`. This is the status quo on [azure.github.io](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk-for-python/ref/azure.identity.html).

Defining classes in a "private" module is an easy way to prevent that. I plan to move all the credentials into `_credentials`.

(socks.SOCKS5, host, port)

And Telethon will translate that into the following:

```python
aiosocks.Socks5Addr(host, port)

Cython's docs recommend [memoryviews](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/userguide/memoryviews.html) over the old `np.array` API. 

I haven't looked through the rest of the PR but if `mask` is a boolean array you can use it without conversion by constructing a view `mask.view(np.uint8)` beforehand which is a compatible dtype to `bint[:, :, :]` (or `np.uint8_t[:, :, :]`, same thing memorywise!). Cython's memoryview API can't yet deal with boolean arrays automatically. That should be the most efficient way.

If you need even faster indexing and your input arrays are contiguous take a look at [this section](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/userguide/memoryviews.html#c-and-fortran-contiguous-memoryviews).

```python
def _add_with_prepended_names(prefix, graph_to_add, destination_graph)
```

That way we can avoid the overhead of constructing the temporary graphdef that's returned here, only to then copy its nodes and functions into the destination graph def. Given that we no longer use `torch.Tensor._make_subclass`, we could also drop the `required_grad` here. 

On one hand we don't expect users to set `requires_grad=True` given that the features are inputs rather than parameters. Plus, even if someone needs this, a simple `.requires_grad_(True)` call is sufficient.

On the other hand, we would diverging from the other tensor creation ops. For example, the ubiquitous [`torch.tensor`](https://pytorch.org/docs/master/generated/torch.tensor.html) takes `requires_grad`. Still, it also takes the `pin_memory` parameter, which we currently also don't support.

All values for type constants are defined in the Java language specification and thus not necessary to look up.  When i was pushing things arround the lookup got lost.

In the case of float we are actually implementing the wrong behavior here as I discovered in the testing.  Unlike ints where overflow makes sense,  floats are supposed to overflow to infinity when the go out of range.  

When i went to test it I discovered that some paths hit thus overflow test and other paths mapped to infinity.  When i tried to make it more consistent more tests broke so we appear to expect different behaviors in different locations.  Looking at the language specification it appears mapping to infinity is correct.  Thus this code appears to be buggy.  

I am fine with either but making this check appear on the other paths is slower, harder to achieve, and no correct according to the language spec.  Thus i recommend silently mapping to inf on out of range.  If you would like it to raise overflow we need some clear rules on the check and not just arbitrary.  

Ints have similar issues on the arbitrary application of overflow.  We test on construction of a wrapper type but not on an array assignment.

The root cause is complex:
- This policy used to be a SansIOPolicy, but got converted to regular policy when challenge was added
- To be backward compatible with someone possibly subclassing it, we kept the on_request/on_response/on_exception methods you would have gotten by inheriting SansIOPolicy. This also means that the implementation of `send` now has to take care of calling those.
- This was implemented incorrectly compared to what the AsyncSansIORunner is actually doing. On_exception never returned a boolean, and so checking if it was handled or not is not a thing. Also, on_response and on_exception are mutually exclusive in the reference implementation, while the code as it was written could have executed both.

As this was added for backward compat and nobody realized we were actually not backward compatible, I would tend to assume that no-one tried to subclass this and relied on SansIO contract.

So for the discussion on "inside" VS "outside" constructing a dead letter path:

- If user is using `ServiceBusClient` to create DLQ receiver, then "inside" VS "outside" is just a matter of internal implementation.

- If user is creating DLQ receiver directly by creating `ServiceBusReceiver`, then "inside" VS "outside" matters.
    - "outside" means we need to provide path generation helper method, user would use the helper method to generate a DLQ path first, then manually pass the path into the receiver.
    - ”insde" means we provide options on the constructor and do the work for users.

So my concern is the "outside" approach definitely brings clarity, but is the clarity that users do care about (want to learn about) -- do they really want to know the detail of how a (Transfer)DLQ path is composed or they just want an option which allows them to connect to DQL queue.

I put my comment on the first occurrence of this return of `False`, but it is valid also for the other occurrences for list, update and delete.

Returning `False` may not be the best thing to do when something wrong happened during the registration of a new record. Indeed there is no strong behavior associated to this (eg. non zero exit code, or overall exception raised by the client). It is just a boolean returned in the output.

It seems to me that here your provider gives the capability to initiate a post validation of the record, and that failing the validation is a real issue (bad input, duplicates etc.).

So instead I would recommend you to create a subclass of the `LexiconError` exception, specific to your provider, like `PostValidationError` and raise it with relevant information given by the API response. This way it becomes a proper failure with non zero exit code on the cli, and it is a catcheable exception for integrators.

```python
def execute_process_with_env_vars_interpolated(execute_process_request, env_to_command):
  with temporary_directory() as tempdir:
    with open(os.path.join(tempdir, "script"), "w") as f:
      lines = ["{key}={value}".format(key=key, value=value) for key, value in sorted(env_to_command.items())]
      lines.append("exec " + shlex(execute_process_request.argv))

    captured_directory_digest = capture(tempdir, "script")

````

There's a weird rule we follow: any code outside of the `_core/` directory is not allowed to "reach inside" the `_core` namespace to access private attributes. (Meaning, attributes with leading underscores.)

The rationale is: we want to make sure that if someone needs to implement something like `from_thread.*` in a third party library on top of trio, then that should be possible without using any private APIs. Everything in `_core`'s public API is re-exported as part of `trio`'s public API, so if we can implement our own stuff using only `_core`'s public API, it means other people will be able to do the same. There's a bit more discussion here: https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/design.html#a-brief-tour-of-trio-s-internals

In this case, that means we can't import `_entry_queue`. But, it looks like the only thing we use it for is to look up the `TrioToken` class object, and that's already exported from `_core`, so we can just do `from ._core import TrioToken` directly.

I moved _changed to be a member variable rather than local to avoid the overhead of allocating it on the GPU every time update is called. The reason it's now a shared_ptr to a bare array instead of a vector is that bool vectors have an optimised implementation that packs multiple bools into each byte, so you can't get a pointer to the raw data, which the GPU requires.

I agree that std::min and std::max would be more readable, but surprisingly the GPU doesn't support them (I was using them initially but they caused weird bugs that took a long time to track down). We could implement custom, GPU-friendly min and max functions rather than writing out the algorithm though.

The generic `except....raise` here is unnecessary. Python will already do the right thing (raise the exception) if it isn't captured by an existing `except` clause. For example:

```py
In [1]: x = {}

In [2]: try:
   ...:     x["a"]
   ...: except ValueError as e:
   ...:     print("nope")
   ...:     
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
```

The `KeyError` is still raised as you would expect.

Here's the use cases, it seems the core still need to know existence of other network interfaces.
* The backbone agent need to know the index of the backbone link interface.
* The Thread border agent should be listening on all network interfaces(Wi-Fi, Ethernet and so on) so that external commissioners can reach the agent through all PHYs.

Actually, in core/net/ip6, whether a datagram comes from the Thread network interface is also checked([code](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/net/ip6.cpp#L1161)).

Besides, one motivation of platform UDP was to allow developers develop their apps/services directly on OpenThread if they find OpenThread's infrastructure is easier to write the code(For example, OpenThread has a good CoAP implementation) and the apps/services may be operating on other network interfaces just like the Thread border agent. Another motivation is trying to integrate OpenThread's IP stack with the host's, so that if OpenThread has a socket bound to 1000, the host should not bind to that port again and vice versa.

I am not sure about moving this logic in a method in `Mac::Frame` class. I suggest we keep it in `Mac` class (as before).
- `Mac:Frame` class is a representation of a frame.
- Make sense for it to provide methods to parse the frame, and get/set different fields in the frame.
- Doing such checks that are tied to whether `aRxOnWhenIdle` does not fit well here.
- The `aRxOnWhenIdle` is really a `Mac` state variable and `Frame` should not be aware of it or care about it. 

I understand that you moved this this here so that it can be reused from `examples/platform/utils` library code. I think the `platform/util` code is behaving more like MAC layer itself than just frame parser.  In this case, I would argue that having a clean model in what `Mac::Mac` class should do, and what `Mac::Frame` class should provide,  is more valuable over maybe not being able to share a bit of code between platform util  library and OT core.

Okay I investigated this for a long time today, and @OniOni checked it out on Friday.

I think I found why `./pants` is using both unicode and bytes for options: Python 2 stores attributes as a map between byte strings to the corresponding values. 

```python
# in Py2
>>> [type(x) for x in dir("test")]

[<type 'str'>, ...]
```

I resolved the issue down to running `./pants` with the following diff:

```diff
diff --git a/src/python/pants/option/option_value_container.py b/src/python/pants/option/option_value_container.py
index 3e6d824ca..c5fb50c12 100644
--- a/src/python/pants/option/option_value_container.py
+++ b/src/python/pants/option/option_value_container.py
@@ -120,6 +122,8 @@ class OptionValueContainer(object):
 
   # Support attribute setting, e.g., opts.foo = 42.
   def __setattr__(self, key, value):
+    if isinstance(key, bytes):
+      key = key.decode('utf-8')
     if key == '_value_map':
       return super(OptionValueContainer, self).__setattr__(key, value)
     self._set(key, value)
@@ -128,6 +132,8 @@ class OptionValueContainer(object):
   # Note: Called only if regular attribute lookup fails,
   # so method and member access will be handled the normal way.
   def __getattr__(self, key):
+    if isinstance(key, bytes):
+      raise ValueError(key)
     if key == '_value_map':
       # In case we get called in copy/deepcopy, which don't invoke the ctor.
       raise AttributeError(key)
```

I do not think it makes sense to force the `OptionValueContainer` to use unicode always, because that's not how Py2 works. Instead, we keep Py2 as is, and this ends up not even being an issue in Py3 because Py3 represents attributes as unicode strings.

I will look for the first queue client I have sitting around in here which very well could be `this.queueClient` and then I will use it to do some action that a task has requested. This is now a scope escalation because I didn't use the client with limited scopes. I would feel much safer if we just didn't ever generate a non-limited client in here.

In the documentation, the `exec-maven-plugin` assumes a success-code of `0`. The successCodes are only used if the values are something other than 0.

https://www.mojohaus.org/exec-maven-plugin/exec-mojo.html#successCodes

`Exit codes to be resolved as successful execution for non-compliant applications (applications not returning 0 for success).`

So, in this case, since we are checking for zero anyway, the `successCodes` line is not needed.

To be clear, I'm not talking about taking out the diff check itself, just the successCodes line:

```
<configuration>
              <executable>diff</executable>
              <commandlineArgs>${project.build.directory}/custom-javah/org_apache_mxnet_init_native_c_api.h ${project.parent.basedir}/init-native/src/main/native/org_apache_mxnet_init_native_c_api.h</commandlineArgs>
 </configuration>

Instead of caching the stats for all stages on the `DatasetStats` object, could we turn `DatasetStats._summarize_blocks()` into a static method or utility function in this module that takes in the dict of stats for a stage? I.e. instead of
```python
self._calculate_blocks_stats(stage_name, metadata, is_substage=False)
blocks_stats_str = self._summarize_blocks(stage_name, is_substage=False)
```
it would be
```python
block_stats = self._calculate_blocks_stats(stage_name, metadata, is_substage=False)
blocks_stats_str = DatasetStats._summarize_blocks(block_stats, is_substage=False)
```

There are a few pros to this approach:
1. No new state added to the `DatasetStats` object, and we avoid adding new state mutation to the class; this keeps the `DatasetStats` object's data model simple.
2. Lifecycle of the stage stats dict is tied directly to its use, instead of being tied to the `DatasetStats` object (which will usually live a lot longer than the stats dict's use).
3. The `_summarize_blocks` contract is more explicit (block stats dict --> summary string vs. stage name --> internally look up block stats dict --> summary string); this should also make this easier to unit test, if so desired.

The only con that I can think of is the calculation of the stats dict object not being cached on repeated calls, but I don't think that's a concern (shouldn't be all that expensive, and repeated calls shouldn't be typical).

I guess it will depend how we are handling our documentation about practices.
For this question, where should our current practices be found by a new contributors? In the documentation folder/webpage or in the active proposals folder/webpage?

For example in numpy (which have done an amazing job, thanks for pointing to them in your proposal), they have the proposal page where there is only one active proposals which is about how to write a proposal. Applying `PEP8` is part of their [developer documentation](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/index.html).

So we could have a proposal to apply `PEP8`, which become final when all the code has been updated to follow the style, and the developer documentation updated to include this fact (maybe even a check in the CI that all the pushed code is compliant :thinking:) . From then, the source of truth for a contributor is the main documentation.

Otherwise, a contributor will have to look at the documentation AND the active proposals to know the repo practices.

But as you're rightly pointing out, we currently do not have any documentation in the anonlink repo (as sorely internal). So should all our practices be made available from the active proposals, or from a future documentation?

```
NOTE By mutual agreement of the partners in information interchange, the character [T] may be omitted in applications where there is no risk of confusing a date and time of day representation with others defined in this International Standard.

It sort of is a transformation, in a sense. The trace represents one possible linearization of the execution, but there are many others that would also be valid and could be produced with a slightly different adapter implementation. "Linearized" here is (perhaps non-obviously) referring to the property of [linearizability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linearizability) — the trace is a concrete linearization of the observed execution, a "proof by construction" that the interpreter satisfies the linearizability property. Linearizability isn't that important at the moment (the interpreter executes synchronously on a single thread) but becomes more interesting if/when we add `async` support.

For "actuation of iterables", you're right that it is partially covered by "adapter function yields", but it's not entirely the same — the `data_contexts` input argument to adapter functions is also actuated, but in that case the adapter function is _yielded to_. This operation is also recorded in the trace, and I felt it was worth mentioning here since the previous class' docstring wasn't explicit about that since it doesn't distinguish between the two kinds of yields.

I initially removed this to avoid gathering dependencies up-front in favor of forcing all dependencies to be awaited. I was trying to make it so that when creating an `Output`, you'd only pass a single up-front Resource as a dependency, and the only way to get the final set of all dependencies would be to await it.

`gatherDependencySet` was being called to gather up-front dependencies and it looked like the implementation of `awaitInputs` would have picked up most of the nested dependencies anyway, so that's why I initially removed it. However, `gatherDependencySet` does at least one thing that `awaitInputs` doesn't: if a nested value is a `Resource` the resource value is added to the set of dependencies.

In any case, I decided to add back `gatherDependencySet` to avoid missing the resource case. Now, you can continue to pass an array of up-front dependencies you know about when creating an `Output` (as is currently the case), and when you await it, you'll get a merged set of all dependencies (up-front + any other dependencies that came back as part of resolving the output).

With `from .extensions import ...` (whether wildcard or not), you effectively make extension classes implemented in `django_ca.openssh.extensions` available under the shorter and nicer `django_ca.openssh`. That's all well and good, no complaint about that in general.

You have a similar layout in `django_ca.extensions`. Try disabling one of the members in `__all__` in `django_ca.extensions.__init__` and run mypy against the source code. In this example, I've disabled the `KeyUsage` export:

```
$ mypy --strict ca/django_ca
ca/django_ca/models.py:82: error: Module "django_ca.extensions" does not explicitly export attribute "KeyUsage"; implicit reexport disabled  [attr-defined]
ca/django_ca/forms.py:31: error: Module "django_ca.extensions" does not explicitly export attribute "KeyUsage"; implicit reexport disabled  [attr-defined]
...
```

So that's why the `__all__` should be in `__init__.py`. (I believe pylint will also complain).

When you have a wildcard import, e.g. flake8 will not be able to catch some errors. Star imports are thus itself a flake8 warning, and any variable used from those star imports will also throw one.

import sys

>>> sys.excepthook = lambda x, y, z: 1/0

Error in sys.excepthook:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <lambda>
ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

Original exception was:
Traceback (most recent call last):

Exception

    def _excepthook(self):

        try:

        finally:
            self.capture_exception(exec_info, handled=False)
```

This file is meant to be fully frozen, so only `==` are allowed. You could try just  bumping the version of that library alone, but it probably won't "just work" as it might have updated dependency constraints in the newer version, or other frozen libraries might have constraints on that library. Since we have no tests for this other than waiting for rtd do use it, we can't tell if this "works" until after merging.

Proper procedure (that ideally would be automated) would be to use something like `pip-tools` to bump versions of all libraries in this file and verify that this works by building a python environment with updated version.

A manual way would be to
1. Build python environment with unchanged file
2. Update library of interest, verify that doesn't break other libs (boto* will probably break), update those libs as needed
3. Dump new frozen set, taking care of things like `pkg-resources==0.0.0` lines
4. Commit the file to git

The difference has to do with ownership and mutability. If the object is returning one of its members, it should return by constant reference (`const std::vector<T>&`) to avoid returning a new copy of the data, while also ensuring that the returned reference is immutable (constant) so that the calling code cannot mutate this object's internal state. If the vector being returned is not a member (for example, if it is allocated and filled by the function being called) then you must return `std::vector<T>` -- note that this cannot be constant because it is a returning by copy (or move if the copy is elided), and it cannot be a reference because the data's lifetime is limited to the function body and the reference would be invalid.

```python


# Extend the IDToken model so that we can manually tweak the migration

class IDToken(AbstractIDToken):
    """
    We have to override this model because mysql cannot handle indexes on a
    TextField, and the standard migrations will break when run on mysql.

    By overriding the field and marking it as swapped for this modedl, Django
    won't attempt to run the migration for the base model and we can use our
    hand crafted migration to create the table as best we can.
    """

    pass


# Create a custom DB migration operation to do things differently on mysql

class MysqlOnlyRunSQL(migrations.RunSQL):
    """
    Mysql cannot create indexes on a whole text field, and django will not auto
    restrain the index to what mysql does support.

    We can hack this in a way that adds what mysql can support with a RunSQL
    migration, but that will fail on non mysql databases. This class wraps RunSQL
    and if the migration is run on a non mysql database, it will instead run the
    operations specified in the state_operations.

    This allows django to think that the desired state has been achieved on both
    mysql and non-mysql databases.
    """

    @property
    def reversible(self):
        """
        Can only be reversed if both the RunSQL and regular operations can be reversed
        """
        return self.reverse_sql is not None and all(
            op.reversible for op in self.state_operations
        )

    def database_forwards(self, app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state):
        """
        If we are using a mysql schema editor, run the supplied SQL.
        Otherwise, apply the operations in state_operations
        """
        if "mysql" in schema_editor.__class__.__module__:
            super().database_forwards(app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state)
        else:
            for op in self.state_operations:
                op.database_forwards(app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state)

    def database_backwards(self, app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state):
        """
        If we are using a mysql schema editor, run the supplied SQL.
        Otherwise, apply the operations in state_operations
        """
        if "mysql" in schema_editor.__class__.__module__:
            super().database_backwards(app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state)
        else:
            for op in self.state_operations:
                op.database_backwards(app_label, schema_editor, from_state, to_state)


# Handcraft the initial migration

        migrations.CreateModel(
            name="IDToken",
            fields=[
                ("id", models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True, serialize=False)),
                ("token", models.TextField()),
                ("expires", models.DateTimeField()),
                ("scope", models.TextField(blank=True)),
                ("created", models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)),
                ("updated", models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)),
                (
                    "application",
                    models.ForeignKey(
                        blank=True,
                        null=True,
                        on_delete=django.db.models.deletion.CASCADE,
                        to=oauth2_settings.APPLICATION_MODEL,
                    ),
                ),
                (
                    "user",
                    models.ForeignKey(
                        blank=True,
                        null=True,
                        on_delete=django.db.models.deletion.CASCADE,
                        related_name="pots_oauth_idtoken",
                        to=settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL,
                    ),
                ),
            ],
            options={
                "abstract": False,
            },
        ),
        # Hack to add partial indexes only on mysql, and full indexes on other DBs
        MysqlOnlyRunSQL(
            "CREATE INDEX `token_idx` ON `pots_oauth_idtoken` (`token`(255))",
            reverse_sql="ALTER TABLE `pots_oauth_idtoken` DROP INDEX `token_idx`",
            state_operations=[
                migrations.AlterField(
                    model_name="IDToken",
                    name="token",
                    field=models.TextField(unique=True),
                ),
            ],
        ),

I briefly looked at that approach, thinking that it would be nice if we could have a single `ObjectDirectory` implementation that took an implementer of `ObjectInfoAccessor`, either GCS-backed or owner-core-worker-backed. There were a few reasons I decided to not go that route (for now):

1. The simplest short-term ownership-based object location subscription implements a different protocol: logically versioned snapshots of the location state, instead of location state update events. I couldn't immediately see a good interface for `ObjectInfoAccessor::AsyncSubscribeToLocations` that could support both protocols. This is something that I'd like to reexamine once we support gRPC server-side streaming in Ray.
2. The `ObjectInfoAccessor` is currently a subinterface for the GCS client, and decoupling _just_ that accessor from the GCS client seemed like it could be messy. The other option would be pulling out all of the accessor subinterfaces into a sibling `accessor` module (or the like), allowing any easy path for those interfaces to be implemented by core workers and/or raylets as more of the GCS state becomes decentralized.
3. I thought that there should be a broader discussion around refactoring the core worker client and raylet client interfaces to adopt something closer to the GCS client model, consisting of application-level subinterfaces that are mostly decoupled from the gRPC layer and whose implementers do the application-level <--> transport level conversion and make the underlying RPC calls, making the users of those subinterfaces mostly transport layer agnostic. I personally like that model, but it'd be a large refactor.
4. It was my impression that the general design of the OBOD was agreed upon, and I didn't want to come in towards the end of the implementation and rewrite all of it. 😄 

(1) and (3) were my biggest reasons for not running with that route. Do you think this is something that we should reexamine once server-side streaming is supported in Ray, or do you think this is something that we should figure out sooner (not in this PR obviously, but in the short-term)?

Also, for the prefix ("`cuda_`" rather than "`awkward_`"), the reason we have a prefix is to avoid collisions in a namespace—suppose that a user is also writing `cupy.RawKernel` and they use the same names. If we don't have actual C++ namespaces with "`::`", we use a prefix. (The cpu-kernels have a C interface, so no namespaces.)

  1. Perhaps you want the prefix to be "`awkward`", rather than "`cuda`", since you're trying to disambiguate these functions from other CUDA functions, but not from other Awkward functions. (One of the benefits of a library with an odd name is that no one else is likely to pick the same thing.)
  2. Can you use real C++ namespaces now that we're JIT-compiling?

For the automated mapping of names, your starting point is what the kernel calls are doing:

```python
nplike["name_of_kernel_without_specialization", np.uint32, np.int64](arguments)
```

In your bookkeeping, you need to get from a kernel name without specialization plus the dtype of every array (pointer-based) argument passed into or out of it. If you arrange your kernels to be templated on all the pointer-based arguments, regardless of whether it needs to be (you might have only one specialization), then the mapping can be automated.

This function is a bit hard to spec. The IR only talks about fields, and the query builder interprets them as columns in complicated ways. This function is a bit ugly since it tries to guess what the query builder will need. I have two different fixes in mind:
a) Instead of using sqlalchemy selectables as the main building block for queries in this module, make a new mutable selectable class that keeps track of what columns are requested of it, and can be materialized into a sqlalchemy selectable that exposes only those columns. Then this function wouldn't need to exist.
b) Document in the spec of this function what is considered a "used column", and then make sure that:
  - all objects in `CompilationState._aliases` expose all the used columns
  - only used columns are selected from `CompilationState._aliases`

Solution (a) fixes code duplication and removes some source of bugs, but it's too complicated to implement and will likely introduce other kinds of problems. Solution (b) seems right, even though it doesn't solve the duplication problem. It just creates a chain of specs from definition of "used column" to usage of "used column" that it might make this more manageable.

I suspect properly specifying this function will reveal some bugs. For instance, it's a bit unclear whether meta fields produce "used columns", and whether using a meta field inside recurse would cause a problem. I'd do this in a separate PR though, let me know if you agree.

@leanaha Great question. Since this is an `abstractproperty` (https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/abc.html#abc.abstractproperty), it _must_ be overridden by inheriting classes otherwise those classes cannot be instantiated:

> A class that has a metaclass derived from [ABCMeta](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/abc.html#abc.ABCMeta) cannot be instantiated unless all of its abstract methods and properties are overridden.
https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/abc.html#abc.abstractmethod

You can still manually call an abstract method or property using the `super()` method but in this case you'll already have defined a `session` property anyway.

> How easy would it be to add a new column to state? (Would it be better to use an ORM or create migrations instead -- but that's even more complex, haha). 

Yeah, I thought about using an ORM / creating migrations, but I wanted to stay with something as simple as possible (this actually also motivated my use of sqlite, I'll mention more below). I don't think adding an extra column to the state is too difficult. The JSON state manager gets around this by just clobbering the existing state with whatever format it wants to write. We could do the same in the sqlite case, if we see that the existing table schema doesn't match what we expect. It's not ideal, but I think it gets around the issue fine without adding too much complexity.

Also, FWIW, blame seems to say that the state schema hasn't changed in several years. I don't want to say that it won't change in the future, but I historically it seems pretty infrequent—might be worth taking into consideration

> Are you sure we wouldn't be fine if we just added mutexes to make the process of reading from + writing to the JSON state file thread-safe?

No, I'm not sure :) I think that's a fine solution to the issue too. When I was first thinking of how to fix this issue, I was going to do something like this, but then I realized that sqlite has (1) basically already solved this problem, and (2) put a lot more effort into solving it robustly, so I opted for that instead. I thought that it'd be simpler to just import and use sqlite instead of building a lock system.

I think this is correct. @simonlingoogle can you please help double-check my reasons below (these are always tricky and good to have multiple eyes on them).

`aName` can be a single label or a dot separated name like `"label1.label2.label3"`. In the second case `aName` length can indeed be longer than `mLabelLength`.

- This statement is there to  check that the `aName` length is longer than `mLabelLength`
  - so that we can safely use `CompareBytes()` to read and compre `mLabelLength` bytes from `aName` (and avoid reading beyond the end of the string).
- Or saying it another way, this statement ensures that in the first `mLabelLength` chars of the `aName` array, there is no null char. 
  - `StringFind(aString, aMaxLength)` checks the first `aMaxLength` chars in the string char array against null char (e.g.,`StringLengrth(aString, 1)` would check only one index at `aString[0]`).
  -  So `StringFind(aName, mLabelLength)` checks the first `mLabelLength` chars in `mName`.

Please notice that even a SLAAC address is expired/deprecated, it doesn't stops the application layer to use it:
>Note that the feasibility of initiating new communication using a
   non-deprecated address may be an application-specific decision, as
   only the application may have knowledge about whether the (now)
   deprecated address was (or still is) in use by the application.  For
example, if an application explicitly specifies that the protocol
   stack use a deprecated address as a source address, the protocol
   stack must accept that; the application might request it because that
   IP address is used in higher-level communication and there might be a
   requirement that the multiple connections in such a grouping use the
   same pair of IP addresses.

The original issue (#350) was raising a `KeyError` instead of a `TypeError` so this wouldn't catch the correct problem, but also simply printing "TypeError" is not how this should be handled. But this is probably the right place to make a fix.

I suggest doing an explicit type check and raising a `TypeError`. E.g.,

```python
        if not isinstance(datum, str):
            raise TypeError('interact() argument must be a string, '
                            f'not {type(datum).__name__!r}')
        validated = self._validate_input(datum)
```

In this case I think the `TypeError` is the correct error type to raise, instead of a custom exception class. Also, with this `isinstance()` check here, the one in `ACEParser._validate_input()` becomes redundant. It's not wrong to leave it there, but it's slightly less efficient.

If you need to modify values that would live in the context variables and you need to
make those modifications from the child threads, you can instead set a mutable object
(e.g. a dictionary) in the context variable of the top level/parent Trio thread.

object, and modify its values. That way you keep the same object in the context
variable and only mutate it in child threads.

new content in the parent thread.

Here's a different implementation, like I did it [in MkDocs](https://github.com/mkdocs/mkdocs/blob/56b235a8ad43f2300d17f87e6fa4de7a3d764397/mkdocs/livereload/__init__.py#L166).

It has the benefit of being instantly stoppable, rather than having a 0 - 0.2 sec delay before being able to stop.

It also ensures to instantly stop firing events when it's stopped, maybe  eliminating the need for
> The more involved start and stop mechanisms in AutoRestartTrick are needed to avoid restarting the debouncer when restart the process

```suggestion
import logging
import threading


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class EventDebouncer(threading.Thread):
    """Background thread for debouncing event handling.

    When an event is received, wait until the configured debounce interval
    passes before calling the callback.  If additional events are received
    before the interval passes, reset the timer and keep waiting.  When the
    debouncing interval passes, the callback will be called with a list of
    events in the order in which they were received.
    """
    def __init__(self, debounce_interval_seconds, events_callback):
        super().__init__()
        self.debounce_interval_seconds = debounce_interval_seconds
        self.events_callback = events_callback

        self._cond = threading.Condition()
        self._shutdown = False
        self._events = []

    def handle_event(self, event):
        with self._cond:
            self._events.append(event)
            self._cond.notify_all()

    def run(self):
        while True:
            with self._cond:
                while not self._cond.wait_for(lambda: self._events or self._shutdown, timeout=0.2):
                    # We could have used just one wait instead of a loop + timeout, but we need
                    # occasional breaks, otherwise on Windows we can't receive KeyboardInterrupt.
                    pass
                if self._shutdown:
                    break
                if self.debounce_interval_seconds:
                    while self._cond.wait(timeout=self.debounce_interval_seconds):
                        # Wait for events to stop happening.
                        pass

                events = list(self._events)
                self._events.clear()

            self.events_callback(events)

    def stop(self):
        with self._cond:
            self._shutdown = True
            self._cond.notify_all()
```

I'm pretty sure this sort of copying back the context can be used to allow things run by `run_in_threadpool` to behave like if they were being called directly (which would solve issues FastAPI has with sync dependencies)

While it is interesting to consider this "high level" logic for the client to optimise back and forth and have a ready to use RC client, the problem is deeper and lower level.

Consider this small bit of Ruby code:

```ruby
# fork.rb
class Foo
  attr_reader :foo, :bar

  def initialize
    @foo = []
    @bar = []
  end

  def update(a, b)
    # these two updates should be atomic to have a consistent state
    @mu.lock
    # here state is consistent
    @foo << a
    # here state is now inconsistent
    sleep 1 # artificially inflating time so that breakage reliably happens
    @bar << b
    # here state is consistent again
  ensure
    @mu.unlock
  end
end

foo = Foo.new

t1 = Thread.new do
  foo.update(1, 2)
end.run

sleep 0.5 # artificially controlling time so that breakage reliably happens
fork do
  puts "child"
  p t1
  p foo.instance_eval { @mu.locked? }
  p foo.foo
  p foo.bar
end

t1.join

puts "parent"
p foo.foo
p foo.bar
```

`ruby fork.rb` outputs:

```
child
#<Thread:0x0000000102fd0c98 fork.rb:18 dead>
false
[1]
[]
parent
[1]
[2]
```

Since `fork` is not under our control it can happen anytime, including during a state update, and irrespective of any lock.

The state-updating thread is killed, and the child internal state ends up being inconsistent, thus _cannot_ be relied upon. By internal state this may be the RC repository content, but also any other mutable state of the client; this may involve not just state that we set but language-internal stuff that could be interrupted or altered. Notice that e.g locks are also affected, as the mutex in the child is suddenly unlocked; This is the case for Ruby as it ensures forked mutexes are unlocked to be helpful (otherwise they would never be unlocked since the thread is dead in the child).

The quick and general purpose way out of it is to simply create a fresh new client and dispatch the old one.

To tackle the state carryover improvement, there could be other possibilities:

- resetting the forked client to a known valid state. This means creating some form of overall transaction mechanism over any mutable state, that would be set atomically WRT the `fork` call, and be able to be rolled back atomically as well.
- creating a new client but passing it known-consistent initialisation state. This means being able to save such state atomically WRT the `fork` call so that it can be passed later on.

Yes. That's intentional and I think is correct. This is idea behind the existing code. Each "attach attempt" iteration consists of 3 phases:
- We have the first phase where we send "Parent Request to routers only" and wait for a response.
- Followed by the second phase "Parent Request to routers and REEDs".
- If both fails (no response), then we enter the third phase and send Announce messages (which we send over ~1400 msec interval on all channels)
- At the start of this third phase, we also send a third "Parent Request" to add another chance to find a parent.
- During this phase we still process "Parent Response" from any parent and try to attach to it.

One thing to note is If we change the timing here, it can impact overall time a SED device stays in rx per attach attempt and can then impact battery lifetime (we then need to update the attach backoff parameters accordingly)

To me, it feels like the default should be `True`.  I expect that most people are going be interested in pickling unmodified modules, and "just want it to work".  So, if the majority of people will not use the default, then it's likely the wrong default. I'm fine with:

> the whole module may end being saved by reference

because, that is what `pickle` would give you.  I think it's also going to be expected that `dill` should do at least what `pickle` does, and using a default of `False` may give an impression that this is not the case.

If changing the default to `True`, we'd need to:

1. Document that `refonfail=False` may lead to faster serialization, but may fail when something in the module fails to serialize. 
2. Document that modifications will be ignored in any module that has to be stored by reference.

I think this is pretty acceptable, and a better choice.
